risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Ninety patients were randomized 2:1 to the treatment arm using a minimization algorithm in an effort to balance the groups for disease severity. Groups were stratified according to baseline six minute walk test with a threshold of 300 meters and baseline upright forced vital capacity with a threshold of 55% of the predicted value." Comment: A minimization algorithm with stratification is a systematic allocation method consistent with proper random sequence generation; although exact computer/random-number details are not provided, the description is sufficient to judge the sequence as plausibly random and not predictably allocated.

Summary: The report states that "Ninety patients were randomized 2:1 to the treatment arm using a minimization algorithm in an effort to balance the groups" and that this was a "double-blind, multi-center, randomized controlled trial," but it does not describe any concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation, SNOSE, or identical numbered containers). Comment: Randomization method and blinding are reported, but there is no explicit information that would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This study was a post-hoc analysis ... in a double-blind, multi-center, randomized controlled trial" and "the control group received a volume matched placebo infusion." Comment: The trial explicitly states it was double-blind and placebo-controlled with volume-matched placebo infusions, indicating that participants and personnel delivering treatment were likely blinded. The outcomes assessed here (ECG and echocardiographic parameters, plus trial efficacy/safety outcomes) are not obviously susceptible to performance bias from knowledge of allocation, so the overall domain risk is low.

Quote: "All ECG’s were interpreted at the central cardiology core lab by a cardiologist blinded to treatment arm." and "All echocardiograms were interpreted at the central cardiology core lab by a cardiologist blinded to treatment allocation." Comment: The outcome assessors were explicitly blinded, and the outcomes assessed here (ECG and echocardiographic parameters) are objective, instrument-based measurements interpreted using standard criteria, so knowledge of allocation would be unlikely to influence outcome measurement.

Quote: "Five (8%) patients in the enzyme replacement therapy group and four (14%) patients in the placebo group did not complete the study. Reasons for not completing the study in the treatment group included the following: hypersensitivity reactions related to the study medication (2), death unrelated to treatment (1), and switch to commercial enzyme replacement therapy (2). In the control group, the reasons included: adverse event not related to study medication (1), switch to commercial enzyme replacement therapy (1), and missed 78-week study appointments (2)." Comment: Attrition was modest and fairly balanced between groups (8% vs 14%). Reasons were reported and were not clearly related to the cardiovascular outcomes being analyzed; the study also notes that no discontinuations were related to the cardiovascular system. Although the efficacy analysis used only participants with baseline and 78-week data, the overall level and pattern of missingness do not suggest meaningful attrition bias.
